finding: Normal root-key rotation requires a sequential N+1 root authorized by threshold signatures from both the currently trusted root and the incoming root, with each key counted once. Threshold root compromise requires a separately authenticated out-of-band recovery anchor.
design_implication: A state-authority resolver needs an explicit root-policy transition gate before accepting a new authority set. The gate must reject rollback, skipped versions, missing old or new threshold, duplicate signer inflation, expired roots, and unanchored emergency recovery.

covered: exact out-of-band emergency digest plus new threshold

uncertainty: Generated test keys do not model independent authority administration or hardware-backed custody.
The emergency digest channel is represented as trusted input; its real-world authentication is outside this module.
The implementation is TUF-inspired and is not represented as a complete TUF client implementation.
falsification_route: Produce a root transition accepted without the configured old threshold and without an exact emergency digest.
Produce a root transition accepted with a non-sequential or decreasing root version.
Produce threshold acceptance by repeating one key signature.
Produce emergency acceptance when the supplied digest does not match the canonical proposed root.
claim_boundary: Cryptographic acceptance establishes authorization under configured keys and anchors only; it does not establish honest administration, uncompromised custody, or factual correctness of downstream state.
